hero_description: Master three traditional Indian embroidery techniques—Zari (gold thread work), Mirror (shisha embroidery), and Chikankari (white thread work)—in just 8 weeks. This foundation course equips you with hands-on skills to create stunning embellished garments and textiles for the thriving Indian fashion market.
overview_description: This comprehensive embroidery program covers the fundamentals of Zari work (metallic thread embroidery), Mirror embroidery (shisha/glass bead attachment), and Chikankari (delicate white thread work). You'll learn fabric preparation, design transfer, stitching techniques, quality control, and practical production methods used in Indian fashion houses, wedding wear, and export industries. Perfect for aspiring embroidery artisans, tailors, and home-based entrepreneurs.

modules.title: Introduction to Embroidery & Textile Fundamentals
topics: History and cultural significance of Zari, Mirror, and Chikankari embroidery in India — Understanding regional variations (Lucknow, Gujarat, Rajasthan)
Types of embroidery fabrics — Cotton, silk, chiffon, georgette, net, dupatta, lehenga materials and their properties
Fabric grain, weave structure, and thread count — How fabric quality affects embroidery output
Introduction to embroidery tools and equipment — Needles, hoops, scissors, thimbles, measuring tools, and their correct usage
Safety and hygiene in embroidery work — Eye care, posture, workspace organization, and ergonomic practices
Quality standards and defect identification — Common embroidery faults and how to prevent them
Indian embroidery market overview — Wedding wear, ethnic clothing, export opportunities, and pricing models
Introduction to design reading and pattern understanding — Basic embroidery design symbols and layouts

modules.title: Zari Embroidery Techniques & Metallic Thread Work
topics: Understanding Zari materials — Gold, silver, copper threads, artificial zari, real zari composition, thickness grades (1mm, 1.5mm, 2mm)
Types of Zari threads — Flat zari, twisted zari, round zari, and their applications in different garments
Zari needle selection and threading techniques — Correct needle sizes (sizes 5-8), thread conditioning, and knot formation
Basic Zari stitches — Straight stitch, backstitch, split stitch, running stitch, and long and short stitch using metallic threads
Couching technique for Zari work — Laying zari threads and securing with fine thread, creating outlines and fills
Zari border designs — Creating traditional borders for dupattas, sarees, lehengas, and suits using couching methods
Padding and dimensional Zari work — Building height and texture for wedding embroidery, creating raised designs
Zari combined with beads and stones — Attaching kundan, rhinestones, and pearls alongside zari for bridal wear
Common Zari defects and corrections — Thread breakage, color fading, tarnishing prevention, and storage methods
Zari work on different fabric types — Techniques for silk, georgette, chiffon, and net fabrics

modules.title: Mirror & Shisha Embroidery Techniques
topics: Introduction to Mirror embroidery (Shisha work) — Origin, cultural significance in Rajasthan and Gujarat, mirror types and sizes
Mirror materials and preparation — Glass mirrors, acrylic mirrors, thickness selection, cutting, and edge finishing for safety
Mirror attachment methods — Traditional frame stitch, modern loop stitch, cross stitch method for securing mirrors to fabric
Decorative stitches around mirrors — Chain stitch, blanket stitch, running stitch, and herringbone stitch for aesthetic finish
Mirror work on different fabrics — Techniques for cotton, velvet, silk, and ethnic fabrics used in Indian wear
Creating mirror work patterns — Geometric designs, floral arrangements, and traditional motifs for suits, dupattas, and ghagras
Combining mirrors with other threads — Using silk thread, metallic thread, and beads around mirrors for enhanced designs
Mirror work for wedding garments — Bridal lehenga designs, mirror placement for maximum impact, and durability considerations
Mirror work troubleshooting — Preventing mirror breakage, loose attachment, and maintaining design integrity during wear and washing
Production efficiency in mirror work — Speed techniques, batch processing, and time management for commercial orders

modules.title: Chikankari Embroidery — White Thread Traditional Technique
topics: Understanding Chikankari — Origin in Lucknow, cultural heritage, regional variations, and its role in Indian ethnic fashion
Chikankari thread types — White thread grades, cotton thread quality, thread thickness for different stitches, and durability
Chikankari on traditional fabrics — Muslin, cotton, linen, chiffon, and georgette used in Lucknowi wear
Basic Chikankari stitches — Backstitch (pechni), running stitch (murri), satin stitch (bakhia), chain stitch (zanjira), and French knot
Advanced Chikankari stitches — Eyelet work (tepchi), shadow work, cutwork (khidai), and lattice work (jali)
Design transfer for Chikankari — Tracing methods, carbon paper technique, and freehand design marking on white fabrics
Creating traditional Chikankari motifs — Floral designs, leaf patterns, geometric borders, and corner pieces for dupattas and suits
Chikankari on curved surfaces — Necklines, armholes, and shaped garment pieces with consistent stitch quality
Combining Chikankari with other techniques — Mixing white thread with colored threads, bead addition, and mirror work integration
Quality standards in Chikankari — Stitch uniformity, thread tension, knot hiding, and finishing techniques for export-quality pieces

modules.title: Design, Fabric Preparation & Pattern Transfer
topics: Reading embroidery designs — Understanding design sketches, color coding, stitch symbols, and scale measurements from pattern sheets
Design adaptation for different garments — Scaling designs for suits, lehengas, dupattas, sarees, and customized pieces
Fabric preparation before embroidery — Washing, blocking, straightening, and marking grain lines for consistent design placement
Marking and measuring techniques — Using rulers, measuring tapes, chalk, pencils, and stencils for accurate design placement
Pattern transfer methods — Tracing paper method, carbon transfer, light box technique, and prick and pounce method for detailed designs
Marking for Zari work — Outlining areas for couching, identifying padding zones, and marking stone/bead placement points
Marking for Mirror work — Positioning mirrors on fabric, marking attachment points, and planning decorative stitch placement
Marking for Chikankari — Light pencil marks for white thread work, maintaining visibility on white fabrics, and erasing marks after completion
Fabric stabilization — Using backing materials, hooping techniques, and support fabrics to prevent puckering and distortion
Design planning for production — Batch marking, efficiency layouts, and minimizing fabric waste in commercial embroidery

modules.title: Finishing, Quality Control & Career Development
topics: Finishing embroidered pieces — Removing backing materials, pressing embroidery safely, and final stitch securing techniques
Knot hiding and thread securing — Professional methods to hide thread ends, secure stitches, and prevent unraveling during wear
Embroidery care and maintenance — Washing instructions, storage methods, and preventing color bleeding and thread damage
Quality inspection checklist — Stitch uniformity, design accuracy, thread tension consistency, and defect identification in finished pieces
Identifying and correcting common defects — Loose stitches, thread breakage, color fading, mirror breakage, and incomplete designs
Production costing and pricing — Calculating material costs, labor time, overhead, and setting competitive prices for embroidery services
Time management and efficiency — Optimizing work speed without compromising quality, production planning, and deadline management
Customer communication and requirements gathering — Understanding client specifications, design preferences, fabric choices, and delivery timelines
Building a portfolio — Documenting completed work, photographing embroidery pieces, and creating a professional portfolio for clients and employers
Career pathways in embroidery — Employment opportunities, self-employment options, entrepreneurship models, and skill advancement to master level
Digital tools for embroidery business — Using mobile apps for order management, design inspiration from Pinterest/Instagram, and online marketing basics
Indian embroidery market trends — Current demand for wedding wear, ethnic fashion, export opportunities, and sustainable embroidery practices

assessment.theory_format: 25 Questions × 2 Marks each (Multiple Choice, Short Answer, and Descriptive)
assessment.practical_format: Candidates must complete three embroidered samples demonstrating Zari, Mirror, and Chikankari techniques with specified design complexity, stitch quality, and finishing standards.

practical_items.title: Zari Embroidered Sample
practical_items.description: Candidate must complete a 15cm × 15cm fabric sample with couched Zari work including straight lines, curved designs, and at least one padded element. Must demonstrate proper thread handling, consistent stitch quality, and professional finishing.

practical_items.title: Mirror Embroidered Sample
practical_items.description: Candidate must attach a minimum of 5 mirrors (various sizes) on a 15cm × 15cm fabric piece using appropriate attachment stitches. Must include decorative stitching around mirrors, proper mirror positioning, and secure fastening.

practical_items.title: Chikankari Embroidered Sample
practical_items.description: Candidate must complete a 15cm × 15cm white thread embroidery piece on light fabric featuring minimum 3 different Chikankari stitches (backstitch, running stitch, eyelet work, or French knots). Must demonstrate stitch uniformity, design accuracy, and professional finishing.

eligibility: Already working as embroidery artisan? Apply for direct RPL (Recognition of Prior Learning) exam
Valid ID proof required at registration (Aadhaar, PAN, Voter ID, or Driving License)
Applicable for all candidates including persons with disabilities (with reasonable accommodations)
Preference given to candidates from weaker economic sections and women entrepreneurs
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate proficiency in Zari embroidery techniques including couching, padding, and metallic thread handling on various fabrics
Execute Mirror/Shisha embroidery with proper mirror attachment, decorative stitching, and design placement on ethnic garments
Master traditional Chikankari stitches including backstitch, running stitch, eyelet work, and French knots with consistent quality
Prepare fabrics, transfer designs accurately, and maintain proper measurements for professional embroidery production
Identify and correct common embroidery defects while maintaining quality standards for commercial and custom orders
Calculate production costs, manage timelines, and communicate effectively with clients for embroidery business operations
Apply safety, hygiene, and ergonomic practices in embroidery work to prevent occupational health issues
Create a professional portfolio and develop a viable business plan for self-employment or employment in fashion industry

career_paths.title: Embroidery Artisan (Home-Based)
career_paths.description: Work from home taking custom orders from wedding wear designers, boutiques, and online clients. Build repeat customer base and scale to hiring assistants.

career_paths.title: Embroidery Workshop Supervisor
career_paths.description: Manage embroidery production in fashion studios, wedding wear shops, and textile units. Oversee quality, train junior staff, and meet client deadlines.

career_paths.title: Wedding Wear Embroidery Specialist
career_paths.description: Specialize in bridal lehenga, saree, and suit embroidery. Work with high-end designers or run independent bridal embroidery studio. High earning potential during wedding seasons.

career_paths.title: Export-Quality Embroidery Producer
career_paths.description: Produce embroidered garments for export to UK, USA, Gulf countries. Meet international quality standards. Supply to fashion export houses and online global platforms.

career_paths.title: Ethnic Fashion Brand Embroidery Lead
career_paths.description: Work with ethnic wear brands (Biba, Libas, Kalki Fashion) managing embroidery design and production. Oversee seasonal collections and quality assurance.

career_paths.title: Online Embroidery Business Owner
career_paths.description: Sell embroidered pieces on Etsy, Amazon Handmade, Instagram, and own website. Create niche products (customized dupattas, saree borders, accessories) with high margins.

career_paths.title: Embroidery Trainer/Instructor
career_paths.description: Teach embroidery in vocational institutes, community centers, or online platforms. Create video tutorials. Conduct workshops for NGOs and skill development programs.

career_paths.title: Garment Factory Embroidery Technician
career_paths.description: Work in large apparel manufacturing units handling embroidery for bulk orders. Ensure consistency, manage inventory, and maintain production schedules.

course_materials_provided: Complete embroidery toolkit (needles, hoops, scissors, thimbles, measuring tools)
Sample fabric pieces (cotton, silk, chiffon, net) for practice
Zari threads in multiple colors and thicknesses
Mirror/shisha pieces (glass and acrylic) in various sizes
Design pattern sheets and tracing materials
Instructional handouts in English and Hindi
trainer_qualifications: Minimum 5 years of professional embroidery experience
Certification in embroidery or textile craft
Knowledge of Indian traditional embroidery techniques
Training certification from recognized vocational body
Ability to teach in English and Hindi/regional language
infrastructure_requirements: Well-lit classroom with natural and artificial lighting
Individual workstations with embroidery frames and seating
Storage for fabrics, threads, and tools
Display area for student work and reference samples
First aid kit for needle pricks and minor injuries
Washing and pressing facilities for finished pieces
additional_information.industry_partnerships: This course is designed in consultation with embroidery units in Lucknow, Delhi, Jaipur, and Ahmedabad. Successful candidates can connect with industry partners for employment or wholesale orders.
additional_information.placement_support: CETCF provides portfolio review, interview preparation, and job placement assistance. Candidates receive referrals to embroidery studios, fashion brands, and export houses.
additional_information.skill_progression: After completing this Foundation course, candidates can pursue Advanced Embroidery Techniques (combining multiple styles), Machine Embroidery Operation, or Fashion Design with Embroidery Specialization.
additional_information.certification_validity: Certificate is valid for life and recognized across India by fashion industry, export bodies, and vocational institutions. Eligible for bank loans and government schemes for self-employment.
